extra.message: SQL injection via string concatenation in SQLinjection.run(). The 'id' parameter from request.params is concatenated directly into SQL string: cursor.execute("SELECT id, username, firstname, lastname, email, session FROM users WHERE id=" + id). No parameterization. Attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing payload like '9999999 OR 1=1' or '9999999; DROP TABLE users;--'

SQL injection in AuthBypass.run() via password parameter. Password is concatenated directly into login query: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"). While username is sanitized with re.sub(), password has no sanitization. Attacker can bypass authentication using payload like ' OR '1'='1 in the password field.

OS command injection in CommandInjection.run(). The 'domain' parameter is concatenated with command name and passed to subprocess.check_output() with shell=True: subprocess.check_output(' '.join([command, domain]), shell=True, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT, stdin=subprocess.PIPE). Attacker can execute arbitrary commands using payloads like 'google.com;whoami' or 'google.com&&cat /etc/passwd'. Requires risk level 3.

cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

extra.message: Insecure deserialization via pickle in UnsafeDeserialization.run(). User-supplied base64-encoded object parameter is decoded and passed directly to pickle.loads(): pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(object)). Pickle deserialization is inherently unsafe as it can execute arbitrary Python code. Attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload with __reduce__ to achieve remote code execution. Requires risk level 3.

Path traversal in PathTraversal.run(). The 'path' parameter is passed to open() after os.path.abspath() which does NOT prevent traversal attacks: file = open(os.path.abspath(path), 'rb'). Attacker can read arbitrary files using payloads like '../../etc/passwd' or '../../../../etc/shadow'. The only check is whether '://' is in the path (which routes to SSRF instead).

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

extra.message: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in PathTraversal.run(). When the 'path' parameter contains '://', it is passed directly to urllib.request.urlopen(path), allowing attacker to make requests to arbitrary URLs including internal services. Attacker can access metadata services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ on AWS), internal APIs, or scan internal network using payloads like 'http://localhost:8080' or 'http://internal-service:9000'.

Critical command injection via custom HTTP method BDR in do_BDR(). The URL path (self.path[1:]) is passed directly to subprocess.check_output() with shell=True: subprocess.check_output(self.path[1:], shell=True, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT, stdin=subprocess.PIPE). Attacker can execute arbitrary commands by sending a BDR HTTP request with the command as the URL path, e.g., 'BDR /whoami'. Requires risk level 3.

cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

extra.message: Path traversal in StaticHandler.find(). The URL path is used directly in file open calls without sanitization: open('./{}'.format(path), 'rb') and open('./{}'.format(path), 'r'). No directory restriction or path normalization prevents traversal. Attacker can read arbitrary files from the filesystem using payloads like '/static/../../etc/passwd.jpg' (with appropriate extension to bypass route matching).

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory
